mnemonics: Compactness: Finite Cover (like a FINite blanket covering a COMPact car). Sequential Compactness: Every sequence has a CONvergent SUBsequence (like finding a good SUBtitle in a long movie). Equivalence: In metric spaces, these two 'COMPany' cars are the SAME model (they are equivalent).
keyPoints: A metric space is compact if and only if it is sequentially compact.
Compactness means every open cover has a finite subcover.
Sequential compactness means every sequence has a convergent subsequence.
This equivalence is a fundamental property of metric spaces, but it does not hold in more general topological spaces.
The proof often uses the concept of total boundedness and completeness.
commonMistakes: Assuming that compactness and sequential compactness are equivalent in all topological spaces (they are not, this is a special property of metric spaces).
Confusing 'every sequence has a convergent subsequence' (sequential compactness) with 'every sequence is convergent' (which is not required).
Forgetting that the limit of the convergent subsequence must be *in the space* itself, not just close to it.
